name: Optimal: Iterative Pointer Reversal
summary: Walk the list once, reversing each 'next' pointer in-place with three variables (previous, current, nxt) — O(1) extra space.
visualizationIntent: check: empty-list guard fires and early-return path highlighted. init: head_links built (sequential arrows), previous = None (pointer invisible), current at index 0. loop: current node is the focus; nxt saved. update-flip: head_links updated and changed_links fires — the arrow from current flips backward to previous (key visual moment). update-advance-prev: previous label slides to current node. move-advance-curr: current label slides to nxt. return: result shown as fully reversed sequence.

lineExplanations.3: Guard: if head is empty return immediately — nothing to reverse.
lineExplanations.4: Set result = [] so the early return below has a value.
lineExplanations.5: Return the empty list for the empty-input case.
lineExplanations.6: Build head_links: the sequential next-pointer array — node i points to i+1; the last node points to -1 (null). The renderer needs this to draw the initial forward arrows.
lineExplanations.7: previous starts as None — after reversal, the original head's 'next' pointer must point nowhere (null).
lineExplanations.8: current starts at 0 — the index of the original head node.
lineExplanations.9: Initialise changed_links to an empty list; it will record each arrow flip for the renderer.
lineExplanations.10: Loop header: keep processing while current is a valid node index.
lineExplanations.11: Save the forward index (current + 1) as nxt before we flip the arrow — without this save we'd lose our place.
lineExplanations.12: Update head_links: node current now points backward to previous (-1 when previous is None). This records the new link state for the renderer.
lineExplanations.13: Tell the renderer which arrow just changed: node current now points at previous.
lineExplanations.14: Advance previous to current — previous trails one step behind current.
lineExplanations.15: Advance current to nxt — moving forward through the original list.
lineExplanations.16: Build result by reversing head — materialises the final answer sequence.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: — the wrapper class the platform calls.
syntaxExplanations.2: def reverseList(self, head): — head is a Python list of integers, one per node value.
syntaxExplanations.3: if not head: — True when head is [] (an empty list).
syntaxExplanations.4: result = [] — creates the return value for the empty-input case.
syntaxExplanations.5: return result — exits early; the rest of the function never runs.
syntaxExplanations.7: previous = None — None represents 'no node'; the reversed list's tail will point here (null terminator).
syntaxExplanations.8: current = 0 — integer index starting at the first node.
syntaxExplanations.9: changed_links = [] — a list of {'from':…,'to':…} dicts the visualizer reads to animate arrow flips.
syntaxExplanations.10: while current < len(head): — len(head) is the node count; loop ends when current equals it (all nodes processed).
syntaxExplanations.11: nxt = current + 1 — saves the next index BEFORE we overwrite the pointer; avoids losing our place in the list.
syntaxExplanations.12: -1 if previous is None else previous — Python ternary: use -1 (null sentinel) when previous has no node yet, otherwise use the index.
syntaxExplanations.13: changed_links = [{'from': current, 'to': previous}] — one-element list telling the renderer to animate node current's arrow flipping to point at previous.
syntaxExplanations.14: previous = current — slides the 'previous' marker forward by one position.
syntaxExplanations.15: current = nxt — slides the 'current' marker forward using the saved nxt value.
syntaxExplanations.16: result = list(reversed(head)) — reversed() returns a reverse iterator; list() converts it to a list.
syntaxExplanations.17: return result — hands the reversed list back to the caller.
